title: Drying handmade sheets
subtitle: Air-drying and board-drying pressed papermaking sheets
excerpt: Once the wet sheet has been pressed, it needs to dry slowly and evenly to end up flat. Rushed drying on a hot surface produces cockling and waviness. The traditional methods, loft-drying on horsehair lines, or board-drying on a smooth surface, both work at home scale with minor modifications.
sourceNotes: Drying methods for hand-pulled sheets documented in Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), public domain, archive.org/details/oldpapermaking00hunt. Loft-drying on lines and board-drying on smooth surfaces are both described as historical practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-drying freshly pressed sheets by hanging or draping them on lines in a warm, well-ventilated room. The traditional finishing stage in the papermaking loft.

Smoothing a freshly pressed damp sheet onto a smooth board and allowing it to dry under slight tension. The sheet shrinks slightly as it dries and releases naturally when fully dry. Produces a flatter sheet than free-hanging drying.

content.text: A well-pressed sheet has lost most of its water. What remains is the water bound within the fibre structure itself, and this needs to evaporate slowly. The sheet is still fragile at this stage: handle it gently at the edges, not in the centre.

At home, loft drying is done by draping sheets over a wooden drying rack, a clothes airer, or a taut line of smooth cotton cord. The sheet should lie over the support, not be clipped or pegged, pegs leave marks. Avoid draping over wire, which also leaves a mark. A warm room with moving air (an open window, not a fan blowing directly at the sheets) dries most sheets in 4, 8 hours.

Sheets dried by lofting often have a slight wave. This is normal for air-dried paper and can be flattened with a brief press under boards once fully dry. Do not put partially dry sheets under weight: they will stick to each other or to the boards.

Board drying produces a flatter result. After pressing, smooth the sheet face-down onto a clean piece of melamine-coated board, polished plywood, or a sheet of glass. Use the sponge to press out any air bubbles and ensure full contact. Leave the sheet to dry naturally on the board. As the fibre dries it contracts slightly, pulling the sheet flat. When fully dry, the sheet releases cleanly from a non-porous surface like melamine or glass.

Do not dry on raw wood: the sheet will stick and tear. Do not speed the drying with a hairdryer: the surface moisture evaporates faster than the inner moisture, which forces the fibres into a buckled surface. Patience and even warmth produce the flattest result.

Once fully dry, the sheet can be flattened further under press boards overnight. Stack the dry sheets interleaved with smooth cartridge paper (to prevent any residual sticking) and weight heavily. If the sheets are intended for writing, the next step is surface sizing with gelatin.
